news_summary: Advancements in human rights include efforts to promote fairness and reduce bias in science job applications by eliminating recommendation letters.
news_summary_fr: Parmi les avancées en matière de droits de l'homme, on peut citer les efforts visant à promouvoir l'équité et à réduire les préjugés dans les candidatures à des postes scientifiques en supprimant les lettres de recommandation.
news_summary_es: Entre los avances en materia de derechos humanos se incluyen las iniciativas para promover la equidad y reducir los sesgos en las solicitudes de empleo en el ámbito científico mediante la eliminación de las cartas de recomendación.

articles.title: Why it’s time to bin recommendation letters in science job applications
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 01 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00507-xIf hiring organizations must ask for references, they should do so closer to the end of the process, argues Bibek Aryal.
articles.content:
Requiring many letters of recommendation at the first stage of a job application can sink jobseekers’ chances.Credit: frema/iStock via Getty

Late last year, a colleague showed me a job posting that he was interested in. The research aligned perfectly with his training, the laboratory was prestigious and the timing was right for his next career move.

But he let the application deadline pass by without submitting, when he realized he needed to supply three letters of recommendation.

A reference from his current supervisor, the most relevant person to speak about his recent work, was not something he felt able to ask for. He had already exhausted his supervisor’s goodwill on previous applications and further straining the relationship for yet another request was not viable.

The opportunity disappeared, not because he lacked the skills, but because he felt that he could not clear this procedural barrier.

His story is not unusual. It plays out constantly in labs around the world because of a hiring practice that has outlived its usefulness: requiring several letters of recommendation at the first stage of a job application.

As a physician-scientist who has worked in Japan, the United States and southeast Asia, I have watched this problem repeat itself across borders and institutions. The reference paradox, as I have come to think of it, punishes vulnerable researchers, rewards those with privileged connections and shrinks the talent pool of science.

A system built for a different era

Fifty years ago, letters of recommendation made sense. They verified that candidates were real, had completed their training and possessed the skills that they claimed. Hiring committees had few other ways to confirm these facts. If you were looking for a scientist with experience in mouse microsurgery and received an application, then a signed reference letter was one of the only ways to verify their experience.

But that world no longer exists. Today we have Google Scholar, ORCID, PubMed and databases of institutional credentials that store records of academic and professional achievements. A hiring committee can verify a candidate’s publications, citation metrics, grant history and clinical experience in minutes. The verification function of letters of reference has been entirely replaced by digital transparency.

But in many places, we’ve kept the ritual anyway. Perhaps this is because, for many search committees, a personal endorsement from a peer still offers a perceived layer of security that digital metrics alone cannot match.

Dear editors, your publication delays are damaging our careers

During my training, my professional goals didn’t always match my supervisors’ expectations. They imagined me embarking on a clinical career in my home country, whereas I was intent on exploring fellowship opportunities abroad. Asking for a recommendation letter meant exposing that divergence prematurely. For many in this position, the result is a withdrawal; individuals simply decide the application isn’t worth the risk of friction.

The invisible cost

The true damage of this requirement lies in careers that never launch and talent that we never discover.

Put yourself in the shoes of a junior researcher who has spotted an ideal position. Before you can even be considered, you must ask senior colleagues to write letters — signalling your intention to leave. If your supervisor is difficult, the relationship has soured or they even like you too much to let you go, this request becomes fraught with risk, because a lukewarm reference can quietly sink chances.

Even in healthy, supportive labs, the system creates problems. Academic job markets are brutally competitive, with researchers often applying for dozens of positions. Returning to the same supervisor to share news of rejection after rejection is psychologically exhausting and humiliating. Candidates begin to worry: “Am I becoming a burden? Do my mentors see me as someone who cannot succeed?” Even now, as an independent cancer-outcomes researcher and liver surgeon, when I see an intriguing position that requires letters of recommendation at the initial stage, I often close the browser tab. The prospect of reopening a conversation and taking a mentor’s time for a mere application is a price I am frequently unwilling to pay.

Eventually, many of us simply stop trying and remain in our current roles, sacrificing potentially greater impact elsewhere for the predictable comfort of the status quo.

Filtering for privilege, not merit
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the negative impact of requiring early-stage recommendation letters in science job applications, which disproportionately harms vulnerable researchers and rewards privileged connections. It argues for reforming this outdated practice to create a fairer, more merit-based hiring process that could broaden access and opportunity in scientific careers globally.
articles.personality_title: Rethinking recommendation letters in science job applications
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many science job applications ask candidates to provide several letters of recommendation right at the start. These letters are meant to support the candidate’s skills and experience but can create problems for applicants.

**What happened** – Bibek Aryal, a physician-scientist, explains how requiring recommendation letters early in the hiring process can stop talented researchers from applying. He shares a story about a colleague who missed a great job opportunity because he didn’t want to ask his supervisor for another letter, fearing it would harm their relationship.

**Impact** – This old practice favors people with strong connections and makes it harder for others to compete fairly. Today, digital tools like Google Scholar and PubMed already show candidates’ work and achievements clearly. Asking for letters too soon can discourage good applicants, reduce diversity, and keep many skilled scientists stuck in their current jobs.

**What's next step** – Aryal suggests that if references are needed, they should come later in the hiring process, after candidates pass initial checks. Changing this could make science jobs fairer and open more doors for researchers worldwide.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Asking for recommendation letters only at later stages can help science job hiring become more fair and focused on real skills, not just personal connections.
articles.personality_title_fr: Repenser les lettres de recommandation dans les candidatures scientifiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreuses candidatures à des postes scientifiques demandent plusieurs lettres de recommandation dès le début. Ces lettres sont censées soutenir les compétences et l’expérience du candidat, mais peuvent poser des problèmes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Bibek Aryal, médecin-chercheur, explique que cette exigence peut empêcher des chercheurs talentueux de postuler. Il raconte l’histoire d’un collègue qui a raté une belle opportunité car il ne voulait pas demander une autre lettre à son superviseur, craignant de détériorer leur relation.

**Impact** – Cette pratique ancienne favorise ceux qui ont des relations solides et rend la compétition difficile pour les autres. Aujourd’hui, des outils numériques comme Google Scholar montrent déjà clairement les travaux des candidats. Demander des lettres trop tôt peut décourager de bons candidats, réduire la diversité et bloquer des chercheurs compétents dans leur poste actuel.

**Prochaine étape** – Aryal propose que les références soient demandées plus tard, après les premières vérifications. Ce changement pourrait rendre les recrutements plus justes et ouvrir plus de portes aux chercheurs dans le monde.

**Une phrase à retenir** – Demander les lettres de recommandation seulement en fin de processus rendra les recrutements scientifiques plus justes et basés sur les compétences réelles, pas seulement sur les relations personnelles.
articles.personality_title_es: Repensar las cartas de recomendación en solicitudes científicas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas solicitudes para trabajos científicos piden varias cartas de recomendación desde el principio. Estas cartas deben apoyar las habilidades y experiencia del candidato, pero pueden causar problemas.

**Qué pasó** – Bibek Aryal, médico e investigador, explica que esta exigencia puede impedir que investigadores talentosos apliquen. Cuenta la historia de un colega que perdió una gran oportunidad porque no quiso pedir otra carta a su supervisor, por miedo a dañar su relación.

**Impacto** – Esta práctica antigua favorece a quienes tienen conexiones fuertes y dificulta la competencia para otros. Hoy, herramientas digitales como Google Scholar ya muestran claramente el trabajo de los candidatos. Pedir cartas demasiado pronto puede desanimar a buenos candidatos, reducir la diversidad y mantener a investigadores capacitados en sus puestos actuales.

**Próximo paso** – Aryal sugiere que las referencias se pidan más adelante, después de las primeras revisiones. Este cambio podría hacer que las contrataciones sean más justas y abrir más oportunidades a investigadores en todo el mundo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Pedir cartas de recomendación solo en etapas finales puede hacer que la contratación científica sea más justa y basada en habilidades reales, no solo en conexiones personales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a delicate balance scale where one side holds a stack of heavy, tangled recommendation letters weighing down a hopeful, glowing seedling symbolizing emerging talent, while the other side is light and open with transparent digital data streams—like glowing lines and icons—flowing freely around the seedling, set against a soft, simple background of an academic lab with subtle symbolic elements of microscopes and books rendered abstractly.
